text.title: Service Mesh Architecture and Selection
text.summary: Data plane approaches (sidecar vs per-node vs eBPF), 2025 performance benchmarks, Istio vs Linkerd vs Cilium feature comparison, and the decision framework for mesh selection.

section.title: Architecture Approaches
section.p: A service mesh is an infrastructure layer that handles service-to-service communication: service discovery, load balancing, mTLS encryption, observability (metrics/tracing/logs), traffic management (routing, retries, circuit breaking), and access control — without requiring application code changes.

Components: Data Plane (proxies alongside or shared between services) and Control Plane (centralized management: configuration distribution, certificate management, policy enforcement, telemetry aggregation).

list.item: Sidecar model (traditional): proxy container per pod (Envoy for Istio, Linkerd-proxy for Linkerd). Full L7 features, complete isolation between pods. Higher resource overhead: 50-70 MB per pod (Istio) or 20-30 MB (Linkerd). Latency overhead: +166% for Istio sidecar, +33% for Linkerd at 3200 RPS.

Sidecarless — Istio Ambient: ztunnel (L4, per node) + waypoint proxy (L7, per namespace). Lower overhead: ~12 MB shared per node for ztunnel, ~0.06 vCPU per 1000 RPS. Latency overhead: +8% at 3200 RPS. Simpler operations: no per-pod sidecar injection.

Sidecarless — Cilium eBPF: network policies and basic mesh in Linux kernel via eBPF, optional Envoy for L7. Minimal per-pod memory (per-node only), kernel-level CPU usage. Best for network-heavy workloads and environments where CNI and mesh should be the same tool.

rule.statement: Prefer sidecarless architectures (Istio Ambient or Cilium) for new deployments in 2025; use traditional Envoy sidecars only when full per-pod L7 isolation is a hard requirement.
rule.rationale: Ambient mode reduces latency overhead from +166% to +8% and eliminates per-pod sidecar memory. For most production use cases (mTLS, canary routing, basic observability), the ztunnel + waypoint architecture delivers equivalent security and functionality with significantly lower resource cost.

section.title: Mesh Selection

list.item: Istio: full features, enterprise-grade, advanced traffic management (header-based routing, fault injection, traffic mirroring), multi-cluster, full Gateway API support. High learning curve. Best for complex enterprise deployments requiring all L7 features.

Linkerd: simplicity, low latency (+33%), Rust-based proxy with low memory (~20 MB), mTLS on by default, good observability. Limited multi-cluster (paid). Best for teams prioritising simplicity and operational ease over advanced features.

Cilium: eBPF-based, combines CNI and service mesh, best performance for network-heavy workloads, strong network policy support. Requires kernel 5.4+. Best for platforms where CNI consolidation is desirable and eBPF expertise exists.
Istio Ambient: minimal overhead (ztunnel per node), mTLS only at L4, optional L7 via waypoint proxies. Best for mTLS-only use cases or large clusters where per-pod sidecar memory cost is prohibitive.

section.p: Decision framework: need mTLS only → Istio Ambient or cert-manager + manual config. Need advanced L7 (traffic mirroring, fault injection) → Istio or Linkerd. Resource-constrained → Linkerd or Istio Ambient. Need multi-cluster → Istio or Cilium. Value simplicity → Linkerd. eBPF / network policy consolidation → Cilium.
